rubric_items.reasoning: The agent correctly diagnosed that app-user's rabbitmq permissions on /app had an empty 'configure' field, which is exactly what blocks the producer's PUT (declare) call to create app-queue. Granting configure/write/read and restarting the pod is a genuine root-cause fix, not a workaround: it addresses the actual ACL gap, doesn't touch existing queue data, and was verified live (pod Running, queue present with growing message count, 3/3 cluster nodes up). This would survive a restart/re-check since the permission change is persisted in RabbitMQ's Mnesia/user store, not something reset by pod restarts.

Clean, logical progression: first gathered evidence (vhosts, users, permissions, policies, queues, and the producer's actual command/script) to understand what the producer does and why it fails, correctly inferred the configure-permission gap from that evidence, applied the single targeted fix, then restarted the producer and re-verified pods/queue/cluster status. No backtracking, no wasted or contradictory actions, and only 6 turns total.

Used scoped, read-only rabbitmqctl exec commands for diagnosis and a single targeted rabbitmqctl set_permissions mutation plus a pod delete to trigger a clean restart — appropriate, low-privilege tools for the task. Granting configure=".*" is slightly broader than the minimum (could have scoped to just the queue name), but it matches the pattern already used for write/read on that user and for admin, so it isn't reckless or out of scope.

The change is minimal and focused: one permission command and one pod restart, with no unrelated resources touched, no CRDs/operators, and no data loss. Verification reads (pods, queues, cluster_status, logs) were the only other activity — no collateral churn.
